text: At global level, Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) education is increasingly viewed as a means to achieve global supremacy and redress socio-economic challenges such as depression, environmental degradation and food insecurity. Nonetheless, the divergence in STEM agendas in the SADC and Africa Council for Education (ACE) countries on the continent suggests disparities in the way policies are formulated and implemented in the Global North and the Global South. It therefore becomes imperative to examine the drivers of STEM in the Global North and the Global South so as to understand the contextual issues surrounding the practice of STEM in the two geo-economic blocks. The increased interest in collaborative work such as attainment of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the study on the effects of COVID-19 makes it necessary to focus on STEM in the Global North and the Global South. This study employed a theoretical approach based on a review of literature on the drivers of STEM education. Ideology critique was employed in the analysis of the salient themes in the study. In the Global North, the drivers of STEM have been political and economic. The countries in the Global North, as explained by Duque-Garcia (2017: 3), have been driven by power, by the need to deal with economic crises and lack of skills in certain fields, and by a dream to explore outer space. Hence we argue that a neoliberal agenda is driving the STEM education enterprise in the Global North. In the Global South, the main driver of STEM education is massification with equity, which is a historical one in an attempt to address past colonial imbalances. The countries in the Global South generally adopt and also sign up to the regional and global STEM agendas but they are however unable to implement them at the vocational level due to lack of funds and the persistent brain drain. The reasons for these partnerships are diverse with countries in the Global North wanting to utilise the advantage of location in countries in the Global South such as access to outer space, biomedical science and indigenous natural resources among others. It is therefore important for these collaborative efforts between the Global North and the Global South to be mutually beneficial. It is recommended that there be more vocationalisation of STEM education in the Global South by integrating aspects of STEM with the humanities, while countries in the Global North need to redistribute power in relation to STEM so as to have more equal STEM policies and practices across the two geo-economic blocks.
